threat-dashboard · guided walkthrough

A threat hunting, detection and management desktop app — Zig + Vulkan + Dear ImGui, with dockable panels, F-key workspaces, a fuzzy command palette, and crash-safe layout persistence. This guide tours every workspace with captures taken from a live run.

● live capture 2026-07-07 · commit fd25b65 Vulkan · RTX 5090 · 1400×900 mock world seed 42 · 2826 events · 85 alerts · 46 rules · 606 IOCs

00 · Overview

What you're looking at

threat-dashboard is a single-binary SOC console. Five workspaces (bound to F1–F5) each arrange a preset of dockable panels: alert triage, event hunting, detection engineering, threat intel, and fleet operations. Every panel has a three-or-four-letter code — ALQ EVT RUL — that you can type into the command line to jump anywhere.

Out of the box it runs against a deterministic mock world: diurnal telemetry plus scripted incident chains (phish → macro → PowerShell → C2 → credential dump → lateral movement → exfil), so every panel is populated and cross-panel links tell coherent stories. Point it at PostgreSQL and panel actions write back to the database instead.

  • 19 panels across five groups — Triage, Hunt, Detect, Intel, Ops — each rendered from one file in src/panels/.
  • Windows-native: Vulkan renderer, win32 persistence paths, Zig 0.16 toolchain.
  • Keyboard-first: everything reachable via Ctrl+K, F-keys, and single-key table actions.

01 · Getting started

Build & launch

You need Zig 0.16.0+, the LunarG Vulkan SDK ≥ 1.3.296 with VULKAN_SDK set (Debug builds copy the validation layer next to the exe), and Windows.

# build → zig-out/bin/threat-dashboard.exe
zig build

# build + launch the mock world (seed 42)
zig build run

# unit tests (ui, domain, data)
zig build test

Useful flags

FlagEffect
--seed <u64>Mock-world seed — the same seed produces a byte-identical world.
--state-dir <dir>Where layout.ini + ui_state.json live (default: current directory).
--pg <conn-uri>Load the world from PostgreSQL instead of the mock generator.
--selftestHeadless data-path + layout round-trip self-test (leak-gated).
--validateForce-cycle every workspace and panel for a bounded run, then exit.
--screenshot <dir>--validate plus one PNG per workspace — how the captures in this guide were made.
--window WxH, --dpi-scale, --mailbox, --demoSee --help.

02 · Core concepts

Workspaces, panels, and the command line

Workspaces are dock presets. F1–F5 switch between TRIAGE, HUNT, DETECT, INTEL, and OPS; the tab strip at the top-left of the app shows where you are, and the bell beside it counts open alerts wherever you happen to be.

Panels drag, dock, and tab freely (ImGui docking). Rearrange anything; type RESET to rebuild the active workspace's preset if you dig yourself into a hole, and Ctrl+S to snapshot the layout immediately.

The command line (Ctrl+K or /) is the primary navigation. Type a panel code and press Enter to focus it — opening it as a floating window if it isn't in the current workspace. Fuzzy search works too: type part of a name and Enter runs the top match, with ↑↓ moving the suggestion selection. Press ? anytime for the HELP directory — the full panel/code table and keyboard map, generated from the command registry.

The panel directory

  • PST Posture Summary
  • ALQ Alert Queue
  • CAS Cases
  • TLN Timeline
  • EVT Event Search
  • PRC Process Tree
  • NET Network Connections
  • RUL Detection Rules
  • TUN Rule Tuning
  • ATK ATT&CK Matrix
  • IOC IOC List
  • TA Threat Actors
  • FEED Intel Feeds
  • SEN Sensor Health
  • ING Ingestion Stats
  • LOG Event Log
  • JOB Jobs
  • SET Settings
  • HELP Directory

Codes are tinted by group throughout the app — the same identity colors used here. SET (Ctrl+,) and HELP (?) belong to no workspace; they open by code as floating windows.

Workspace tour · 1 of 5

F1TRIAGE — work the alert queue

The morning-coffee workspace: what's on fire, what's being handled, what's fallen over. PST summarizes open alerts by severity with a 24-hour sparkline; ALQ is the keyboard-first triage table; CAS tracks incidents; TLN shows event volume; SEN flags dead sensors before you trust a quiet queue.

Try it live

  1. Press F1. Read PST first: open alerts by severity, open cases, sensors down, and the triaged counter you're about to move.
  2. Click into ALQ, press Ctrl+F and filter by title or entity; the severity chips (info → crit) narrow the view.
  3. Walk rows with ↑↓. Press A to acknowledge, F to mark false-positive, or C to assign the alert to the case selected in CAS.
  4. Watch the triaged count in PST rise as you close alerts out — false-positives and resolves count as triaged; an ack or case assignment keeps the alert open. Toggle show closed in ALQ to review your trail.

Statuses (NEW → INVEST → closed) and case assignments write through the data store — against Postgres these become immediate UPDATEs.

Workspace tour · 2 of 5

F2HUNT — chase the incident chain

The investigation surface. TLN spans the top with a severity-stacked histogram; EVT is the filterable telemetry table beneath it; PRC and NET on the right resolve any event into its process ancestry and network context.

Try it live

  1. Press F2, then click brush in TLN and drag its two handles around a suspicious spike — EVT filters to exactly that window.
  2. In EVT, toggle the kind chips (proc net auth file dns reg script) and use Ctrl+F for a substring match on process or command line — try EncodedCommand.
  3. Press Enter on an event for detail; for chained events, hit open in PRC in the detail pane to jump into the parent → child chain with ATT&CK technique badges.
  4. Scan NET for red rows — destinations matching ip-type indicators from the IOC list.

The mock world scripts full incident chains — phish → macro → PowerShell → C2 → cred dump → lateral movement → exfil — so a brushed spike usually unravels into a story, not noise.

Workspace tour · 3 of 5

F3DETECT — engineer your coverage

Detection engineering in one screen. The ATK matrix maps rule coverage onto MITRE ATT&CK — green enabled, amber testing, grey none, with red counts for open alerts per technique. RUL lists every rule with fire and false-positive stats; TUN ranks the noisiest offenders.

Try it live

  1. Press F3. In ATK, find a technique with a red open-alert count — say T1059.001 PowerShell — and click the cell: RUL filters to the rules covering it.
  2. In RUL, press Enter (or click) on a rule to open its detail — query text, fire count, FP rate — and enable or disable it. Toggles write back to the store.
  3. Open TUN and drag the what-if threshold: suppress rules with FP rate above X — it previews which rules you'd silence and what you'd stop catching.
  4. Grey ATT&CK columns are your blind spots — that's the backlog for new rules.

Workspace tour · 4 of 5

F4INTEL — feeds, indicators, adversaries

The knowledge base. FEED shows each intel feed's sync health and staleness; IOC is the merged indicator table — type, confidence, source feed, and crucially hits, where an indicator actually matched your telemetry; TA profiles named threat actors with their technique chips.

Try it live

  1. Press F4. Check FEED for red status — a stale feed silently ages your whole IOC table. Sync all queues a feed-sync job (watch it run in JOB).
  2. In IOC, filter with the type chips (ip domain sha256 url email) and scan the Hits column — a red hit count means that indicator fired against real events. The red rows in NET come from the ip-type indicators among them — destinations matching an ip IOC light up there.
  3. Select an actor in TA — aliases, motivation, tradecraft notes — and click a technique chip to jump into the ATK matrix at that cell.

Workspace tour · 5 of 5

F5OPS — trust your pipeline

Detection is only as good as ingestion. SEN is a red/amber/green grid of every sensor — EDR, firewall, IDS, DNS, proxy, cloud — with events-per-second, lag, and version; ING charts throughput per sensor kind; JOB runs async work (feed sync, rule backtest, IOC enrichment, retention sweep) with phase, progress, and cancel.

Try it live

  1. Press F5. Anything red in SEN means a sensor is offline — cross-check the bottom status bar, which carries EDR/NET/CLOUD dots on every workspace.
  2. An amber row with high lag means events are arriving late — the CLOUD sensor sits at 198s in the TRIAGE capture's SEN grid (here it hides behind the floating HELP window). Your timeline is already lying to you by that much.
  3. Start a job in JOB and watch phase + progress; jobs are mock async workers in this phase — real ingestion/backtest workers land with the Postgres provider.
  4. Press ? — the floating HELP directory in the capture above — whenever you forget a code or chord.

03 · Reference

Command line & keyboard map

The command grammar has four shapes — panel codes, workspace names, action verbs, and fuzzy search:

You typeWhat happens
ALQ, EVT, RUL…Focus/open that panel (floats if not in this workspace).
TRIAGE HUNT DETECT INTEL OPSSwitch workspace (same as F1–F5).
RESET · SNAP · SEED · DEMORebuild the dock preset · save layout + UI state now · regenerate the mock world with the next seed · toggle the bindings demo.
anything elseFuzzy match against every name; ↑↓ select, Enter runs the top match.

Global

ChordAction
Ctrl+K or /Focus the command line (primary navigation).
F1…F5Workspace TRIAGE / HUNT / DETECT / INTEL / OPS.
Ctrl+Tab / Ctrl+Shift+TabCycle windows/tabs in the focused dock node.
F11Borderless fullscreen toggle.
Ctrl+,SET · Settings.
?HELP directory (Shift+/ outside text inputs).
Ctrl+SSnapshot layout + UI state now (toast confirms).
EscClear command line → close popup → cancel modal. Never confirms.

Tables (focused panel)

ChordAction
Ctrl+FFocus the panel's filter box (ALQ EVT RUL IOC).
↑ ↓Row selection (ALQ EVT RUL CAS).
EnterDefault row action — ALQ ack · EVT detail · RUL toggle detail.
Ctrl+EExport visible rows to CSV (LOG — toast with path).

Alert Queue (ALQ focused)

ChordAction
AAck the selected alert.
FMark the selected alert false-positive.
CAssign the selected alert to the selected case.

Modals

ChordAction
EnterConfirms only after the dwell has elapsed — destructive confirms can't be fat-fingered.
EscAlways cancels.

04 · Data

Mock world & PostgreSQL

The default provider is the deterministic mock generator: same seed + base time ⇒ a byte-identical world (checksum-asserted in the selftest). That determinism is why these captures are reproducible — run the same command and you get the same 2826 events. Type SEED in the command line to regenerate with the next seed, or launch with --seed <n> for a specific one.

Running against PostgreSQL

# one-time: seed a database with a mock world
threat-dashboard pgload --pg postgres://postgres:pass@localhost:5432/postgres

# run against it — panel actions (ack, rule toggles, case moves) write back
threat-dashboard --pg postgres://postgres:pass@localhost:5432/postgres
  • The schema (src/data/schema.sql) is applied idempotently on connect.
  • All panel mutations — alert acks, rule toggles, case moves — fan out through the store's write hook as immediate UPDATEs.
  • If the database is unreachable the app degrades to the mock world with a critical banner — the UI never dies with the DB.
  • v1 PG is deliberately synchronous (connect/migrate/load at boot); a worker-thread live feed is the planned upgrade.

05 · Persistence

Your layout survives everything

ImGui's own ini writer is disabled; the app saves layout.ini itself, crash-safely — written to a temp file and atomically renamed — every 60 seconds when dirty, on every workspace switch, and on exit. ui_state.json keeps the active workspace, mock seed, and filters, so the app reopens exactly where you left it.

  • Drag panels by their tab to dock, split, or float them — every arrangement persists per workspace.
  • RESET rebuilds only the active workspace's preset; the other four keep your customizations.
  • Ctrl+S (or SNAP) forces a save right now; a toast confirms.
  • --state-dir relocates both files — useful for per-profile or throwaway sessions.

06 · Verification

The harness behind this guide

Three built-in verification modes make the app self-demonstrating:

  • --selftest — headless data-path and layout round-trip checks, gated on zero leaks, with the mock world's checksum asserted.
  • --validate — boots the full renderer and force-cycles every workspace and panel for a bounded run: a smoke test of the entire UI surface.
  • --screenshot <dir> — validate, plus one PNG per workspace captured off the swapchain.

Every capture in this walkthrough came from one live invocation:

threat-dashboard --screenshot shots
info: screenshot: wrote shots/ws-0-TRIAGE.png (1400x900)
info: screenshot: wrote shots/ws-1-HUNT.png (1400x900)
info: screenshot: wrote shots/ws-2-DETECT.png (1400x900)
info: screenshot: wrote shots/ws-3-INTEL.png (1400x900)
info: screenshot: wrote shots/ws-4-OPS.png (1400x900)
[validate] rendered 240 frames force-cycling 5 workspaces + 19 panels -> NO CRASH

That last line is the point: the walkthrough you just read doubles as the app's smoke test.

● EDR ● NET ● CLOUD threat-dashboard · guided walkthrough · captured live 2026-07-07 · seed 42
